relevant_files.role: Main SecurityScanner implementation with scan_file and scan_diff methods
Finding, PatternDefinition, ValidatedFinding types; Severity/Confidence enums
Regex-based pattern engine with embedded patterns.json
14 security patterns (hardcoded secrets, SQLi, XSS, etc.)
Smart detection logic for triggering scans

conventions.commits: Conventional commits with GPG signing and DCO signoff
conventions.testing: Unit tests inline, integration tests in tests/ directory, benchmarks in benches/
conventions.linting: cargo clippy with pedantic warnings, cargo fmt for formatting
conventions.error_handling: thiserror for library errors, anyhow for applications; #[derive(Error)] patterns

patterns: Pattern matching via PatternEngine with embedded JSON
Context window around findings for LLM validation
SARIF output for integration with security tools

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection
related_issues.relevance: Primary issue - proposes tree-sitter evaluation for multi-line detection

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Documents regex limitation that tree-sitter would solve

related_issues.title: PR #736 - merged test documenting limitation
related_issues.relevance: Shows test confirming multi-line SQLi not detected

constraints: Workspace with 4 crates: aptu-cli, aptu-core, aptu-ffi, aptu-mcp
No tree-sitter dependency currently - would add new dependency
Must maintain backward compatibility with existing regex scanner
test_coverage: Comprehensive: unit tests inline in each module, integration tests in tests/security_integration.rs (with multi-line limitation documented), benchmarks validating performance target, security_fixtures/ with safe and vulnerable code samples

library_findings.relevant_api: Parser, Language, Tree, Node types; parse() returns Tree; cursor() for traversal
library_findings.notes: Core parsing library; requires grammar crates for language support; tree-sitter-rust for Rust AST

library_findings.relevant_api: language() function to get Language; used with Parser::set_language()
library_findings.notes: Rust grammar enabling Rust-specific vulnerability detection; Issue #737 suggests starting here for SQL injection patterns

library_findings.relevant_api: Regex::new(), is_match(), find() for line-by-line scanning
library_findings.notes: Currently used; simpler but can't handle multi-line patterns; would coexist with tree-sitter approach

approaches.name: Tree-sitter Dual Scanner
approaches.description: Add tree-sitter parser alongside regex scanner; use tree-sitter for multi-line patterns (SQLi, command injection) while keeping regex for single-line patterns (secrets, crypto). New scanner module with language-specific detection.
pros: Can detect data flow (source to sink)
Leverages existing regex scanner for simple patterns
cons: May impact performance if not careful

approaches.name: Hybrid Query Engine
approaches.description: Use tree-sitter queries (S-expressions) for pattern definitions instead of regex; parse code once, run multiple queries. Convert existing patterns.json to tree-sitter queries.
pros: Can express complex multi-line patterns elegantly
cons: Need grammar for each target language

approaches.name: Minimal Tree-sitter Spike
approaches.description: Spike: add tree-sitter + tree-sitter-rust only, implement single multi-line pattern (SQL injection) to prove concept. Keep regex scanner as-is.
pros: Easy to abandon if issues arise
cons: Limited scope may not satisfy all requirements
Additional dependency without immediate full benefit

recommendation: Approach 1 (Tree-sitter Dual Scanner) is recommended because it directly addresses the issue #737 requirements: solves multi-line detection while maintaining backward compatibility. The hybrid approach allows gradual migration - regex for existing patterns, tree-sitter for complex multi-line cases. This aligns with maintainer preference for starting with tree-sitter-rust (per issue). Approach 3 (Minimal Spike) could precede it to validate the approach before full implementation.
